No built-in iTerm2 integration — you open a new iTerm2 window/tab yourself, then attach.

**Steps:**
1. List what's running: `claude-mux -l` (active) or `claude-mux -L` (all, including idle). Session names are the sanitized directory name (no `claude-` prefix).
2. Open a new iTerm2 window/tab (`Cmd+N` / `Cmd+T`).
3. Attach: `claude-mux -t <session-name>` — e.g. `claude-mux -t GSD-T`.

Multiple iTerm2 windows can attach to the same session simultaneously (standard tmux behavior), so you can have several open in parallel.

Caveat: per the guide, `-t` is "user-only, never from inside a session" — run it from a fresh shell, not from inside another claude-mux session. Detach from a session with `Ctrl-b d` (default tmux prefix) to leave it running.
I ran claude-mux ~/projects/GSD-T  It opened the prototype project

Mon: May 11, 2026 12:25 PDT — GSD-T v3.26.11 — CURRENT

That's a bug or fuzzy-matching behavior — `claude-mux <path>` is finding `GSD-T-Prototype` instead of creating a fresh session for `~/projects/GSD-T`. Let me check the project's docs/source to confirm the resolution logic before guessing at workarounds.
How do I turn off a session?

Root cause: **tmux prefix-matching**, not claude-mux fuzzy matching.

claude-mux derives the name correctly (`GSD-T` from the dir basename), but then calls `tmux has-session -t GSD-T`, which **prefix-matches** against `GSD-T-Prototype` and reports "session exists" — so it attaches you to the Prototype instead of creating a new one.

**Fix — rename the older session so prefixes no longer collide:**

```
claude-mux --rename GSD-T-Prototype GSDT-Proto
claude-mux ~/projects/GSD-T
```

Or, if you don't need the prototype session running right now:

```
claude-mux --shutdown GSD-T-Prototype
claude-mux ~/projects/GSD-T
```

There's no `--exact` flag — this is an upstream-tmux semantics issue claude-mux inherits, worth filing as a bug.

For GSD-T workflows, Super I'm finding claude-mux really cumbersome with starting managing these sessions.   I'm now using eye term which has some TMUX integration.  Does it make sense to just abandon claude-mux Or do you see real value with it?

Mon: May 11, 2026 12:27 PDT — GSD-T v3.26.11 — CURRENT

Two distinct things — pick based on intent:

- **Detach (leave it running in the background):** `Ctrl-b d` from inside the session.
- **Shut it down (kill the session entirely):** `claude-mux --shutdown <session-name>` — e.g. `claude-mux --shutdown GSD-T-Prototype`.

To shut down all sessions at once: `claude-mux --shutdown-all` (check `claude-mux --help` to confirm the exact flag name in your version).
OK, Can you shut down claude-mux and detach all it's sessions and tell me what settings to set in Iterm2?  [Image #2]
Shutting down all sessions now.
Can I just update my CC directive to automatically spawn tmux -CC?

All sessions shut down — `0 running` and no tmux server. Clean slate.

---

## iTerm2 tmux settings

Your screenshot looks good already. Here's the recommended config for working with `tmux -CC`:

**Keep as-is (already correct):**
- ✅ **When attaching, restore windows as: `Native windows`** — this is the killer feature; each tmux window becomes a real iTerm tab/window with native scrollback, search, split, and Cmd+T to add windows.
- ✅ **Use "tmux" profile** — lets you give tmux sessions a distinct color/font without affecting normal terminals.
- ✅ **Status bar shows tmux status bar content** — useful so the iTerm status bar reflects tmux's actual state (session/window indicators).
- ✅ **Warn before pausing** — heads-up before iTerm pauses a runaway pane.

**Change one thing:**
- ⬜ → ✅ **Automatically bury the tmux client session after connecting** — turn this ON. Without it, the original "control" tab sticks around showing raw `tmux -CC` protocol chatter. Burying it hides that noise into the session-window picker (`Window → Select Tmux Window`), keeping your visible tabs clean.

**Optional, your call:**
- **Unpause automatically** — leave OFF (current). If a pane gets pause-flagged, you want to know before iTerm spends time catching up.
- **Pausing threshold `120` seconds** — fine as-is.
- **Mirror tmux paste buffer to local clipboard** — turn ON if you want `prefix + ]` paste and tmux copy-mode selections to land in macOS clipboard. Most people want this; off is the safe default.

---

## How to use it

In any iTerm2 window, instead of plain `tmux`:

```bash
tmux -CC                          # new session, native iTerm windows
tmux -CC new -s gsd-t             # new named session
tmux -CC attach -t gsd-t          # reattach to existing
tmux -CC attach                   # attach to last session
```

Once attached, `Cmd+T` makes a new tmux window (= new iTerm tab), `Cmd+W` closes it, `Cmd+1..9` switches windows — all native shortcuts. Detach with `Ctrl-b d` and the session keeps running in the background.

For GSD-T: `cd ~/projects/GSD-T && tmux -CC new -s gsd-t` and you're set.

I currently have:
# Claude shortcuts
alias cc='claude --dangerously-skip-permissions'
export CLAUDE_CODE_NO_FLICKER=1


Can I just prefix the command with Tmux?  I always navigate to the directory before I run cc

Mon: May 11, 2026 12:36 PDT — GSD-T v3.26.11 — CURRENT

Short answer: **don't put `tmux -CC` in your Claude Code directive (CLAUDE.md) — put it in your shell startup or an iTerm profile instead.**

**Why not CLAUDE.md:**
- CLAUDE.md tells *me* (Claude inside an already-running terminal) what to do — by the time I read it, the terminal exists. I can't retroactively wrap it in tmux.
- `tmux -CC` is an iTerm2↔terminal protocol thing, not a Claude thing.

**Three real options, ranked:**

1. **iTerm2 profile command (cleanest)** — Settings → Profiles → General → Command: set to `Command` and enter `tmux -CC new -A -s main`. Every new iTerm window auto-attaches to (or creates) a session named `main`. `-A` means "attach if exists, else create". This gives you native iTerm windows from the very first tab.

2. **Shell rc one-liner** — add to `~/.zshrc`:
   ```bash
   if [[ -z "$TMUX" && "$TERM_PROGRAM" == "iTerm.app" && -z "$VSCODE_PID" ]]; then
     exec tmux -CC new -A -s main
   fi
   ```
   Works for every new shell, but fires inside VS Code/Cursor terminals too unless you guard against them (the `VSCODE_PID` check handles that).

3. **Per-project iTerm profile** — make a separate iTerm profile per GSD-T project with `Command: tmux -CC new -A -s gsd-t -c ~/projects/GSD-T`. Then `Cmd+O` → pick the project. Most ergonomic if you juggle several.

**Recommendation:** option 1 for a single workspace, option 3 if you frequently switch between distinct projects. Skip option 2 unless you live in iTerm exclusively.
I just ran it.  How can I tell if this is a Team X session?

Mon: May 11, 2026 12:40 PDT — GSD-T v3.26.11 — CURRENT

Yes, and that's actually the cleanest approach for your workflow. Update the alias to:

```bash
alias cc='tmux -CC new -A -s "$(basename "$PWD")" claude --dangerously-skip-permissions'
```

**What this does:**
- `tmux -CC` — uses iTerm2 native-window integration
- `new -A` — creates a new session, or attaches if one already exists with that name
- `-s "$(basename "$PWD")"` — names the session after the current directory (e.g. running `cc` in `~/projects/GSD-T` creates session `GSD-T`)
- Trailing `claude --dangerously-skip-permissions` — the command tmux runs inside the new session's first window

**Behavior:**
- Run `cc` in a fresh shell → spawns tmux session named after the dir, launches Claude inside, opens as a native iTerm window.
- Run `cc` again later in the same dir → re-attaches to the existing session (Claude is still running where you left it).
- Run `cc` in a different dir → spawns a separate session for that dir. You can have many in parallel, all as native iTerm windows.
- Close the iTerm window without exiting Claude → session keeps running in the background; next `cc` in that dir picks it back up.

**One caveat:** `tmux -CC` must be launched from a non-tmux shell. If you're already inside tmux and run `cc`, it'll error with "sessions should be nested with care." Add a guard if you want it to fall back gracefully:

```bash
cc() {
  if [[ -n "$TMUX" ]]; then
    claude --dangerously-skip-permissions
  else
    tmux -CC new -A -s "$(basename "$PWD")" claude --dangerously-skip-permissions
  fi
}
```

Function instead of alias so the conditional works. Drop in `~/.zshrc`, replace the existing `alias cc=...` line, `source ~/.zshrc`.

Mon: May 11, 2026 12:44 PDT — GSD-T v3.26.11 — CURRENT

I can check from here — running the diagnostic via Bash will tell us if this Claude process is inside a tmux session.
I have TMux installed.  I want all my GSD-T Iterm2 sessions for all projects to automatically run under TMUX. How do I accomplish that without having to remember to run T-Mucks in each folder?

Good — the alias on line 22 is **partially** there but it's still an alias, not a function. Aliases can't have conditional logic, so the "guard against nested tmux" won't work, and there's no fallback if you're already inside tmux. Replacing it with a function.
If I exit quad will that terminal session still be in tmux

Done. The function is now in `~/.zshrc`.

**To activate it:**

```bash
source ~/.zshrc
```

Or just open a new iTerm tab — fresh shells will pick it up automatically.

**Workflow from now on:**

1. `cd ~/projects/AnyProject`
2. `cc`
3. Claude launches inside a `tmux -CC` session named after the folder (e.g. `GSD-T`, `Tekyz-CRM`), as native iTerm windows.

**Behavior matrix:**

| Where you run `cc` | What happens |
|---|---|
| Fresh iTerm shell, no tmux | Spawns `tmux -CC` session named `<dirname>`, attaches, runs Claude |
| Same dir, second iTerm window | Re-attaches to the existing session — Claude is still running where you left it |
| Different project dir | Spawns a separate session for that dir; you can have many in parallel |
| Already inside tmux | Skips tmux wrapping, runs Claude directly (no nested-tmux error) |

**Quick verify after sourcing:**

```bash
type cc        # should say "cc is a shell function"
```

If it still says "aliased to ...", a different config file is overriding. Let me know and I'll hunt it down.
What if I split my window while in a cloud session?

Tue: May 12, 2026 05:34 PDT — GSD-T v3.26.11 — CURRENT

**No — when Claude exits, the tmux session terminates too.**

Here's why: the way the `cc` function is written, Claude is the **only command** running in that tmux session's first window. tmux's default behavior is to destroy a window when its command exits, and destroying the last window destroys the session. So:

1. You run `cc` → tmux spawns session `GSD-T` with Claude inside.
2. You exit Claude (`/exit` or Ctrl+D twice) → Claude process ends.
3. tmux's only window has nothing to do → tmux kills the window → session ends.
4. iTerm window closes.

**If you want the session to survive Claude exiting** (so you land in a shell prompt inside the tmux session and can rerun `claude` or do other work), I can tweak the function to launch a shell first, with `claude` as the initial command inside it. Two options:

**Option A — keep current behavior (session dies with Claude).** Simpler; matches the mental model "`cc` is just Claude in a persistent wrapper."

**Option B — session survives, drops you to a shell.** Change the function so Claude runs *inside* a shell window:

```bash
tmux -CC new -A -s "$session_name" -c "$PWD" \
  "claude --dangerously-skip-permissions; exec zsh"
```

The trailing `exec zsh` means "after Claude exits, replace this process with a zsh shell" — so the tmux window stays alive with a working prompt.

**Different question — detaching vs. exiting:**

If you instead **detach** (don't exit Claude, just close the window) the session keeps running with Claude alive inside it. Re-running `cc` in the same dir re-attaches. The detach gesture in `tmux -CC` is just closing the iTerm window (or `Ctrl-b d` if you're focused on the control tab).

Which behavior do you want?
How Can I configure I-term to always run inside T-MUX?

Tue: May 12, 2026 05:37 PDT — GSD-T v3.26.11 — CURRENT

Two-step setup — iTerm profile change + a shell guard. The shell guard is the important one because iTerm's "Send text at start" approach is fragile.

**Step 1 — Add a shell-level auto-wrap in `~/.zshrc`**

This is the cleanest mechanism: every new interactive shell that isn't already in tmux re-executes itself inside `tmux -CC`. I'll add it now.
I'm confused by the last sentence.  Each time I launch cloud in a project folder isn't that a dedicated session for that project?

**Step 2 — Reload your config**

```bash
source ~/.zshrc       # in existing tabs (will re-exec into tmux)
# or just open a new iTerm tab
```

**What this does:**

| Scenario | Behavior |
|---|---|
| Open new iTerm tab in `~/projects/Foo` | Auto-launches `tmux -CC` session named `Foo`, lands in shell prompt inside tmux |
| Open new iTerm tab in same `~/projects/Foo` again | Re-attaches to existing `Foo` session (you'll see all prior windows) |
| Open new iTerm tab in `~/projects/Bar` | Spawns separate `Bar` session |
| Already inside tmux, open split | No re-wrapping (the `$TMUX` guard catches it) |
| VS Code / Cursor integrated terminal | No tmux (the `VSCODE_PID` guard catches it) |
| Need a non-tmux shell once | Run `NO_AUTO_TMUX=1 zsh` or open with `env NO_AUTO_TMUX=1 ...` |

**Now `cc` becomes redundant** — since every shell is already in tmux, you can simplify to just running `claude --dangerously-skip-permissions` directly. Want me to flatten the `cc` function so it's just the claude command (since tmux is now ambient)?

**Optional Step 3 — Use the dedicated "tmux" iTerm profile**

You already have "Use 'tmux' profile rather than profile of the connecting session" checked. iTerm auto-creates a profile named `tmux` for tmux-attached panes. You can customize that profile (color, font, status line) separately from your normal shell profile, so tmux windows look visually distinct. Settings → Profiles → tmux.

**One thing to be aware of:** if you launch a shell from a script, cron, or a non-iTerm context (e.g. `ssh` into this Mac, a VS Code task that opens an external Terminal.app), the `TERM_PROGRAM` guard will skip auto-tmux — which is what you want. The guard only fires in iTerm.

Want me to also update the `cc` function to match this new world, or leave it for explicit "I want Claude *and* a dedicated session per project" cases?
